description:
You are acting as the build engineer for a continuous–integration (CI) system.
    A single build log already exists at
    
        /home/user/build_logs/build.log
    
    Your goal is to create a new, filtered report that lists only the lines in the
    original log that record successful uploads of “ *.jar ” artifacts.

    1. Make sure the directory
    
           /home/user/build_logs/
    
       already exists (create it if it doesn’t).
    2. Read the file
    
           /home/user/build_logs/build.log
    
       and, using any standard command-line tool that supports regular expressions
       (e.g. grep, sed, awk, perl, …), extract only those log lines that match the
       following pattern:
    
           Uploaded artifact: <anything ending in “.jar”>
    
       In regex terms that is equivalent to:
    
           Uploaded artifact: .*\.jar
    
    3. Keep the extracted lines in their original order and write them to a new
       file called
    
           /home/user/build_logs/uploaded_jars.log
    
       Append one extra, final line containing an integer summary in the exact
       format shown below (case-sensitive, no extra spaces or punctuation):

           Total JAR uploads: N

       where N is the number of extracted “Uploaded artifact: … .jar” lines.

       • The summary line must be the very last line of the file.
       • Do not include any blank lines anywhere in the new file.

    4. When you are done, display the contents of the newly created
       “uploaded_jars.log” to the terminal so the user can visually verify the
       output.

    The automated test will check ONLY the exact contents of
    “/home/user/build_logs/uploaded_jars.log”.  The file is expected to contain:

        • the original, complete log lines that match the regex
        • followed immediately by the single summary line in the format shown
          above.

    A correct solution therefore requires exactly four lines in the final file:
    three matching log lines plus the summary line.  Any deviation (extra blank
    lines, wrong order, incorrect summary text, etc.) will cause the test to fail.
